explanation:
Git tracks changes to your files like a time machine for your code. Every time you "commit," Git saves a snapshot of your entire project — you can jump back to any snapshot at any time. This beats saving copies like "project_v2_final_FINAL.zip."

The workflow is simple: `git init` creates a new repository, `git clone` copies an existing one. You edit files, `git add` stages the ones you want to save, and `git commit` locks in the snapshot with a message. The staging area is like a loading dock — you pick which boxes (files) go onto the truck (commit).
example:
```bash
# Start a new repo
mkdir my-project && cd my-project
git init

# Create and track files
echo '# My Project' > README.md
git status                # see untracked files
git add README.md         # stage the file
git commit -m "Initial commit"

# Check history
git log --oneline
git diff                  # see unstaged changes

# Clone an existing repo
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git
cd repo
git log --oneline -5      # last 5 commits
```
key_takeaway: The three-step dance — edit, add, commit — is the foundation of every Git workflow.
try_it: Create a file, commit it, modify it, then use `git diff` to see what changed before committing again.

explanation:
Branches are parallel universes for your code. When you create a branch, you fork reality — you can experiment freely without touching the main codebase. Think of it like drafting a letter: you write on a separate sheet, then merge it into the final copy when ready.

`git branch feature` creates the branch, `git checkout feature` switches to it (or `git checkout -b feature` does both). When your work is done, `git merge feature` brings changes back into main. If two branches changed the same lines, Git flags a conflict — you must manually choose which version to keep.
example:
```bash
# Create and switch to a new branch
git checkout -b feature-login

# Make changes
echo 'def login(): pass' > auth.py
git add auth.py
git commit -m "Add login function"

# Switch back and merge
git checkout main
git merge feature-login

# Handle conflicts (when they happen)
git checkout -b feature-a
echo 'version A' > config.txt && git add . && git commit -m "A"
git checkout main
git checkout -b feature-b
echo 'version B' > config.txt && git add . && git commit -m "B"
git checkout main
git merge feature-a    # fast-forward, no conflict
git merge feature-b    # CONFLICT! Edit config.txt, then:
git add config.txt
git commit -m "Merge feature-b, resolve conflict"
```
key_takeaway: Branches let you work in isolation; merging brings changes together. Conflicts are normal — Git tells you where, you decide what.
try_it: Create two branches, make conflicting changes to the same file in each, merge both into main, and resolve the conflict.

explanation:
A remote is a copy of your repo hosted elsewhere — typically on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. The default remote is called `origin`. `git push` sends your local commits to the remote; `git pull` fetches others' commits and merges them into your branch.

Think of it like syncing your phone with the cloud. You can work offline, make commits, and push when you're ready. If someone else pushed while you were offline, `git pull` merges their work. If you diverged too far, Git asks you to resolve conflicts before pushing.
example:
```bash
# Connect to a remote
git remote add origin https://github.com/user/repo.git
git push -u origin main    # -u sets upstream tracking

# Pull latest changes
git pull origin main

# View remotes
git remote -v

# Fetch without merging (safer)
git fetch origin
git log main..origin/main   # see what's new

# Push a branch
git checkout -b feature-search
git push -u origin feature-search

# Delete remote branch
git push origin --delete feature-search
```
key_takeaway: `push` sends your work up, `pull` brings others' work down — `fetch` lets you peek before merging.
try_it: Create a GitHub repo, push your local project to it, then clone it in a different directory and verify both copies match.

explanation:
Rebasing rewrites history to make it cleaner. Instead of a messy merge commit showing where two branches diverged, `git rebase` replays your commits on top of the target branch — like retyping your notes neatly instead of attaching sticky corrections. The result is a straight, linear history.

Cherry-pick is surgical: it grabs one specific commit from another branch and applies it to your current branch. This is useful when a bugfix exists in a feature branch but you need it in main immediately, without merging the entire feature.
example:
```bash
# Rebase: linearize history
git checkout feature-login
git rebase main            # replay feature commits on top of main

# If conflicts during rebase:
# 1. Fix the conflict files
# 2. git add <resolved-files>
# 3. git rebase --continue
# Or abort: git rebase --abort

# Cherry-pick: grab one commit
git log --oneline feature-bugfix   # find the commit hash
git checkout main
git cherry-pick abc1234            # apply that one commit

# Interactive rebase (rewrite last 3 commits)
git rebase -i HEAD~3
# In editor: pick, reword, squash, drop
# squash = combine with previous commit
# reword = change commit message
```
key_takeaway: Rebase creates clean linear history; cherry-pick surgically applies individual commits — both rewrite history, so never rebase published branches.
try_it: Create a feature branch with 3 commits, then use interactive rebase to squash them into one clean commit.

explanation:
A Git workflow is the team's agreement on how branches are used. Without one, developers collide on main and chaos ensues. Two popular models exist: Gitflow uses long-lived branches (main, develop, feature, release, hotfix) for structured releases, while trunk-based development keeps everyone on main with short-lived feature branches merged within a day.

Gitflow suits teams with scheduled releases (mobile apps, enterprise software). Trunk-based suits CI/CD-driven teams that deploy multiple times daily. Both use pull requests for code review. The key rule: never push directly to main in a team project.
example:
```bash
# Gitflow example
git checkout -b develop main          # develop branch
git checkout -b feature/search develop # feature from develop
git commit -m "Add search"
git checkout develop
git merge feature/search
git checkout -b release/1.2 develop   # release candidate
git checkout main
git merge release/1.2
git tag v1.2
git checkout -b hotfix/1.2.1 main     # urgent fix
git checkout main && git merge hotfix/1.2.1
git tag v1.2.1

# Trunk-based example
git checkout -b feature/search main   # short-lived branch
git commit -m "Add search"
git push origin feature/search
# Create PR on GitHub, get review, merge
git checkout main && git pull
git branch -d feature/search

# Pull request workflow
git checkout -b fix/typo
echo 'fix' > typo.txt && git add . && git commit -m "Fix typo"
git push origin fix/typo
# Open PR on GitHub → review → merge → deploy
```
key_takeaway: Pick a workflow and enforce it — Gitflow for structured releases, trunk-based for continuous deployment. The branch strategy is the team contract.
try_it: Simulate Gitflow: create main, develop, a feature branch, merge feature into develop, create a release branch, merge into main, and tag it.

explanation:
Git mastery comes from muscle memory, not memorizing commands. The core patterns are: branching and merging for parallel development, rebasing for clean history, cherry-picking for surgical fixes, and workflow discipline for team coordination. Each problem below reinforces a different aspect of real-world Git usage.

Focus on understanding *why* each command exists, not just *how* to type it. A merge commit preserves the true history of divergence and resolution; a rebase rewrites that history to look linear. Both are correct — the choice depends on your team's contract. Practice on throwaway repos until these decisions feel automatic.
key_takeaway: Git workflows are team contracts — the commands are simple, the discipline to follow the workflow is the hard part.
try_it: Practice on a throwaway repo: create a merge conflict, resolve it, then rebase a feature branch onto main and observe the difference in history.

practice_challenges.title: Revert a Commit Without Losing History
practice_challenges.hint: git revert creates a new commit that undoes the changes from a specific commit without rewriting history.
practice_challenges.description: You accidentally pushed a commit that breaks production. Revert that specific commit while preserving the rest of the history.
practice_challenges.example_input: Commit abc123 introduced a bug in production
practice_challenges.example_output: A new commit def456 that undoes abc123 changes

practice_challenges.title: Resolve a Merge Conflict
practice_challenges.hint: Look for <<<<<<< HEAD markers, edit the file to keep what you want, then git add and git commit.
practice_challenges.description: Two branches modified the same function. Merge them and resolve the conflict by combining both changes logically.
practice_challenges.example_input: Branch A has login() with email validation; Branch B has login() with rate limiting
practice_challenges.example_output: Merged login() with both email validation and rate limiting

practice_challenges.title: Interactive Rebase to Squash Commits
practice_challenges.hint: Use git rebase -i HEAD~N to open an editor where you can squash, reword, or drop commits.
practice_challenges.description: You have 5 messy WIP commits on a feature branch. Squash them into a single clean commit before opening a pull request.
practice_challenges.example_input: 5 commits: WIP, fix typo, WIP2, fix test, final

practice_challenges.title: Cherry-Pick a Hotfix Across Branches
practice_challenges.hint: git cherry-pick <commit-hash> applies the diff from that single commit onto your current branch.
practice_challenges.description: A critical security fix was committed to the develop branch. Apply only that fix to the release branch without merging all of develop.
practice_challenges.example_input: Fix commit abc123 on develop; release/2.0 branch needs it
practice_challenges.example_output: release/2.0 now contains the security fix as a new commit

practice_challenges.title: Recover a Deleted Branch
practice_challenges.hint: git reflog shows every HEAD movement. Find the commit hash where the branch pointed before deletion, then git checkout -b <branch> <hash>.
practice_challenges.description: A team member accidentally deleted a feature branch with 2 weeks of work. Use git reflog to find the last commit on that branch and restore it.
practice_challenges.example_input: Branch feature/payments was deleted; had 15 commits
practice_challenges.example_output: Branch feature/payments restored to its last commit
